findings.issue: AgentStack::run() is 455 lines. It combines model routing, system prompt injection, context initialization, sub-agent forking, plugin hook firing, and the full agent event loop. The large surface makes it difficult to test individual paths, and every new feature risks adding more lines.
findings.recommendation: Extract at minimum three sub-functions: (1) build_agent_loop() — constructs the AgentLoop with resolved provider + config; (2) run_with_loop() — executes the loop and returns the result; (3) handle_subagent() — the sub-agent spawn path (lines ~599-764, already a separate fn but called inline). This would reduce run() to ~100 lines of orchestration.

findings.issue: run_unified() is 325 lines — the single function handles all turn logic, stuck detection, streaming parse, tool dispatch (both read-only and write paths), steering queue polling, and context compaction. Round 1 quality analysis already flagged the lack of tests for this function; its size is the root cause of that risk.
findings.recommendation: Extract: (1) handle_steering_queue() — lines ~900-950 polling logic; (2) handle_stuck() — stuck detector check and response; (3) process_turn_result() — the post-generation result handling. Each extracted function should have its own unit tests.

findings.issue: build_responses_request_body() is 100 lines. It constructs the OpenAI Responses API request body with conditional blocks for thinking config, tool schemas, cache control, and system prompt formatting. The function is difficult to unit-test because it mixes JSON construction with conditional logic.
findings.recommendation: Extract helper functions: build_input_messages(), build_tools_array(), and apply_thinking_config(). Each can be independently tested with known inputs.

findings.issue: BashTool::execute() is 96 lines. It mixes: argument extraction, command classification (install vs normal), backend selection, environment variable filtering, sandboxing path, and result formatting. The dual execution path (sandboxed install-class vs unsandboxed) is easy to break when adding new logic.
findings.recommendation: Split into execute_install_class() and execute_regular() private helpers. The current if is_install_class() branch at line 74 is the natural split point. This aligns with the existing TODO to invert the sandbox model.

findings.issue: DEFAULT_MAX_RETRIES = 3 is defined as a crate-internal constant in providers/common/mod.rs, but all five providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Copilot, OpenRouter) pass the literal `3` to send_with_retry_cb() instead of referencing this constant. If the constant is changed, the providers remain hardcoded at 3.
findings.recommendation: Replace all five `3` literal arguments with DEFAULT_MAX_RETRIES. Search pattern: send_with_retry_cb(request, "...", 3, ...) across openai.rs, anthropic.rs, gemini.rs, copilot.rs, openrouter.rs.

findings.issue: Magic number 2000 (chars) for memory block truncation is hardcoded inline with no named constant and no comment justifying the value. The same magic number 2000 appears independently in crates/ava-praxis/src/synthesis.rs:77 — a duplicated limit with no shared definition.
findings.recommendation: Define const MEMORY_BLOCK_MAX_CHARS: usize = 2000; in ava-types or ava-agent, and reference it from both sites. Add a doc comment explaining the value (e.g., 'empirically determined to fit within a system prompt turn without dominating context').

findings.issue: ProjectState::save() uses map_err(|e| e.to_string()) three times, erasing structured error information. The function returns Result<(), String> instead of a proper error type. Callers cannot distinguish IO errors from serialization errors.
findings.recommendation: Define a ProjectStateError enum (or use AvaError) and return Result<(), ProjectStateError>. Replace map_err(|e| e.to_string()) with map_err(|e| ProjectStateError::Io(e)) and map_err(|e| ProjectStateError::Serialization(e)).

findings.issue: run_post_edit_validation() uses map_err(|error| error.to_string()) erasing structured AvaError type. The function returns Result<ToolResult, String> instead of Result<ToolResult, AvaError>. This propagation is inconsistent with the rest of the codebase which uses ava_types::Result<T>.
findings.recommendation: Change the return type to ava_types::Result<ToolResult> and remove the map_err() call, letting the ? operator propagate the AvaError directly.

findings.issue: Web server startup uses eprintln! for user-facing status messages instead of the tracing framework. Five consecutive eprintln! calls announce the server URL and endpoints. In containerized or log-aggregated deployments, stderr output bypasses structured logging, making these messages invisible to log collectors.
findings.recommendation: Replace the five eprintln! calls with tracing::info! calls. The tracing::info! output routes through whatever subscriber the application configures, keeping web mode consistent with TUI mode's structured logging. The Ctrl+C and shutdown messages can use info! as well.

findings.issue: Two static LazyLock<Regex> at module level use .unwrap() on Regex::new(). While compile-time-validated regex literals are extremely unlikely to fail, the pattern is inconsistent with best practices and could panic at first use if a regex pattern is modified to be invalid during development.
findings.recommendation: Use the regex! macro from the regex crate (which panics at compile time with a better error message), or add a compile-time test: #[test] fn regexes_compile() { let _ = &*RE_DIFF_STAT; let _ = &*RE_ISSUE; } to catch failures during cargo test rather than at runtime.

findings.issue: generate_turn_streaming() is 148 lines and execute_tools_unified() is 159 lines — both are called from the 325-line run_unified(). The three functions form a deeply nested call chain with no clear seam for inserting observability or mocking in tests. A profiling hook or per-turn metric collector would need to touch all three.
findings.recommendation: Add a #[instrument] attribute to generate_turn_streaming() and execute_tools_unified() (if not already present) to get per-span latency data for free. Extract the streaming parse loop in generate_turn_streaming() into a parse_streaming_response() function that can be independently tested with a mock stream.

findings.issue: default_provider() hardcodes 5 failures and 30s cooldown as magic literals inline: Self::new(5, Duration::from_secs(30)). These values appear in tests and documentation but have no named constants, making it easy for test assertions to drift from production values.
findings.recommendation: Define const DEFAULT_FAILURE_THRESHOLD: u32 = 5; and const DEFAULT_COOLDOWN_SECS: u64 = 30; at module level. Reference them in default_provider() and in the test at line 165 that asserts the threshold.

findings.issue: instruction_budget = context_window / 3 uses a magic divisor 3 with no documented rationale. This budget governs how much of the context window is allocated to system instructions. Changing the model's context window will silently change the budget, and the 1/3 split is not obviously correct for all use cases.
findings.recommendation: Define const INSTRUCTION_BUDGET_FRACTION: f64 = 1.0 / 3.0; (or use integer division with a named constant INSTRUCTION_BUDGET_DIVISOR: usize = 3) and add a doc comment explaining the rationale: 'Reserve 1/3 of context for instructions, leaving 2/3 for conversation history and tool results.'
findings.code_snippet: let instruction_budget = context_window / 3;  // no named constant, no comment

findings.issue: App::new() is 142 lines — a constructor that initializes state, registers event handlers, configures theme, loads session, and sets up channels. This makes it difficult to test App initialization in isolation, and adding new initialization steps risks making it even larger.
findings.recommendation: Extract AppState::default_from_config() to handle state initialization, and split channel/event registration into a register_handlers() method. The constructor should ideally be under 50 lines of wiring.

findings.issue: spawn_named() (the sub-agent spawning function) is 166 lines and combines: agent config building, tool registry construction, platform/permission setup, MCP initialization, loop construction, and result packaging. Each of these phases is a distinct responsibility. A bug in any one phase requires reading through all 166 lines.
findings.recommendation: Extract build_subagent_config() (lines ~600-640) and build_subagent_registry() (lines ~640-700) as separate private functions. The spawning logic itself (tokio::spawn + event loop) would then be ~30 lines.
